figures.gloss: The living, divinely designated Imam in unbroken descent from 'Ali and Fatima through Isma'il — for Nizaris today the Aga Khan (the 50th Imam, since 2025); for Tayyibis an Imam in concealment represented by the Da'i al-Mutlaq of the Bohra communities.
figures.facet: Shi'ism's boldest continuity claim kept literal: where Twelvers await a hidden Imam, Nizari Ismailism maintains a PRESENT one — revelation's interpreter alive, addressable, and issuing guidance now.
figures.provenance: Fatimid imamate doctrine (al-Qadi al-Nu'man, Da'a'im al-Islam); Farhad Daftary, The Isma'ilis: Their History and Doctrines; the Aga Khan succession of 2025 is public record.

figures.gloss: Every revelation has an outward letter (zahir) and an inner truth (batin) reachable only through the Imam's authorized interpretation (ta'wil) — the hermeneutic that earned Ismailis the polemical name Batiniyya.
figures.facet: Esotericism as constitutional principle rather than mood: the inner meaning is not free for the finding but keyed to a living interpretive office — hermeneutics and imamate lock into a single doctrine.
figures.provenance: Fatimid ta'wil literature (al-Qadi al-Nu'man, Ja'far ibn Mansur al-Yaman); analyzed in Daftary and in Henry Corbin's studies of Ismaili gnosis.

figures.gloss: The graded missionary organization — da'is, hujjas, and ranks of instruction — that carried Ismailism from clandestine cells across the Abbasid world to an empire: the Fatimid caliphate (909-1171) that built Cairo and al-Azhar.
figures.facet: The rare case of an esoteric hierarchy scaling into a state: the same initiatory ladder that swore in a village disciple administered an empire from Sicily to Sind at its height.
figures.provenance: Fatimid da'wa documented in al-Qadi al-Nu'man and later Tayyibi sources; Daftary, The Isma'ilis; Heinz Halm, The Empire of the Mahdi.

figures.gloss: The Elburz mountain fortress seized by Hasan-i Sabbah in 1090, seat of the Nizari state and its famed library until the Mongols razed it in 1256 — and the site where the Qiyama, the spiritual Resurrection, was proclaimed in 1164.
figures.facet: The most defamed address in religious history: the 'Assassins' of Crusader rumor and Marco Polo's drugged-garden tale were a besieged scholastic community whose real story — hostile sources burned their own — had to be reconstructed from their enemies; the legend, not the order, is the sensation.
figures.provenance: Juwayni's hostile eyewitness account of Alamut's fall; the legend dismantled in Farhad Daftary, The Assassin Legends: Myths of the Isma'ilis; 'hashishiyya' attested as a pejorative label, not a description of practice.

figures.gloss: The anonymous 10th-century Basran encyclopedia — 52 epistles running from arithmetic and music to cosmology and the soul's ascent — framing all the sciences as one ladder back to the One.
figures.facet: Esoteric Islam's bid to own the whole curriculum: number, harmony, and Neoplatonic emanation braided into a single initiatory syllabus — its precise Ismaili affiliation is a long scholarly debate (adopted by the later Tayyibi da'wa, whatever its origin), flagged.
figures.provenance: The Rasa'il themselves (Oxford/IIS critical edition in progress since 2008); affiliation debate surveyed in Daftary and in Nader El-Bizri's introductory volume.

figures.gloss: The da'i who seized the fortress of Alamut in 1090, broke with the Fatimid da'wa in Cairo in 1094 to support Nizar's line after the succession dispute, and founded the Nizari Ismaili state that endured until the Mongol conquest of 1256. He framed the doctrine of ta'lim — that authentic knowledge of God requires the authoritative teaching of the Imam of the time, not independent reasoning.
figures.facet: The historical administrator buried under the legend: near-contemporary sources, including hostile ones, describe an ascetic scholar who reportedly enforced strict law even against his own household, not the hashish-dosed 'Old Man of the Mountain' of Crusader rumor and Marco Polo's tale.
figures.provenance: Juwayni, Tarikh-i Jahangushay (hostile primary account); Farhad Daftary, The Isma'ilis: Their History and Doctrines (1990) and The Assassin Legends: Myths of the Isma'ilis (1994); Marshall Hodgson, The Order of Assassins (1955).

figures.gloss: The chief qadi (judge) of the Fatimid caliphate under al-Mu'izz (d. 974), whose Da'a'im al-Islam ('The Pillars of Islam') became the Fatimid state's official law code, promulgated under the imams' own review; his Asas al-Ta'wil and related works built the framework of esoteric interpretation (ta'wil) the batin/zahir doctrine formalizes.
figures.facet: Law and gnosis fused in a single office: al-Nu'man is simultaneously the state's chief legislator and its chief esotericist, holding together the outward shari'a and the inward ta'wil that elsewhere in Islamic history pull apart into separate, often opposed disciplines.
figures.provenance: Al-Qadi al-Nu'man, Da'a'im al-Islam (ed. and trans. A.A.A. Fyzee, rev. I.K. Poonawala); Ismail K. Poonawala's studies of Fatimid Ismaili literature; Farhad Daftary, The Isma'ilis.

figures.gloss: Persian poet, philosopher, and Fatimid hujja (chief da'i) of Khurasan (1004-c. 1077), converted to Ismailism in Cairo and driven into exile in the remote Pamir region of Badakhshan after his preaching provoked hostility at home; his Safarnama records the seven-year journey to Fatimid Egypt, and his Persian philosophical-poetic corpus remains foundational to Persian Ismaili literature.
figures.facet: An exile who founded a living tradition by being driven out: unable to return home, Nasir Khusraw's Badakhshani refuge became the seedbed of the Ismaili communities of the Pamirs (Tajikistan, Afghanistan, and western China) who revere him as their founding pir to this day.
figures.provenance: Nasir Khusraw, Safarnama (trans. W.M. Thackston, Naser-e Khosraw's Book of Travels, 1986); Alice C. Hunsberger, Nasir Khusraw, the Ruby of Badakhshan (2000); Farhad Daftary, The Isma'ilis.

figures.gloss: Iranian da'i (fl. mid-to-late 10th century, d. after 971) whose Kashf al-Mahjub and Kitab al-Yanabi' ('Wellsprings') built the Fatimid da'wa's Neoplatonic cosmology — a strict apophatic transcendence of God 'beyond being and non-being' (tanzih), emanating first the Universal Intellect and then the Universal Soul, from which the material cosmos descends.
figures.facet: Philosophy conscripted into revelation: al-Sijistani reads Qur'anic cosmogony through a Plotinian grammar so thoroughly that the emanationist ladder — God, Intellect, Soul, Nature — becomes working Fatimid orthodoxy rather than a rival Greek import held at arm's length.
figures.provenance: Abu Ya'qub al-Sijistani, Kashf al-Mahjub (ed. Henry Corbin) and Kitab al-Yanabi' (trans. Paul E. Walker, The Wellsprings of Wisdom, 1994); Paul E. Walker, Abu Ya'qub al-Sijistani: Intellectual Missionary (1996); Farhad Daftary, The Isma'ilis.

figures.gloss: Titled hujjat al-'Iraqayn ('Proof of the Two Iraqs,' d. after 1020), al-Kirmani reworked Fatimid Neoplatonic cosmology in his Rahat al-'Aql ('Repose of the Intellect') into a system of ten separate intellects influenced by al-Farabi, and wrote epistles refuting the Druze deification of the caliph al-Hakim, insisting on the Imam's ordinary humanity.
figures.facet: The da'wa's own guardrail against its own logic: if the Imam mediates hidden divine truth, deifying him is the doctrine's most obvious failure mode — al-Kirmani is the philosopher the Fatimid da'wa produced specifically to hold that line when the nascent Druze movement crossed it.
figures.provenance: Hamid al-Din al-Kirmani, Rahat al-'Aql (ed. M. Kamil Hussein and M. Hilmi); Paul E. Walker, Hamid al-Din al-Kirmani: Ismaili Thought in the Age of al-Hakim (1999); Farhad Daftary, The Isma'ilis.

figures.gloss: The Resurrection (Qiyama) proclaimed at Alamut by Hasan 'ala dhikrihi al-salam on 17 Ramadan 559 (8 August 1164): the inner truth (batin) declared fully unveiled and the outward rule of shari'a lifted for the faithful, who were held to already live within the spiritual reality the Hereafter promises. The doctrine was later re-veiled (satr) under subsequent Nizari leadership as political circumstances required renewed dissimulation (taqiyya).
figures.facet: Esotericism's logical endpoint taken literally, then walked back: if batin ultimately supersedes zahir, Alamut is the one moment in Ismaili history someone actually pulled that trigger — and the community's own subsequent history shows it reversing course rather than living permanently in the antinomian state it announced.
figures.provenance: Juwayni, Tarikh-i Jahangushay (hostile eyewitness account of the 1164 proclamation); Farhad Daftary, The Isma'ilis: Their History and Doctrines; Marshall Hodgson, The Order of Assassins.

figures.gloss: The devotional hymn corpus of the Nizari Khoja (Satpanth) community of South Asia — several hundred hymns in Gujarati, Sindhi, Punjabi, Hindi, and other Indic languages, attributed to a line of pirs (chiefly Pir Sadr al-Din and Pir Hasan Kabir al-Din), sung in jamatkhana worship and transmitted for centuries in oral and Khojki-script manuscript tradition before critical printed editions began in the 20th century.
figures.facet: Doctrine translated rather than merely preached: the Ginans render Ismaili batin/zahir theology, the imamate, and cosmology into Vaishnava-inflected Indic devotional idiom — conversion achieved by making the new faith legible in the convert's own religious vocabulary rather than by displacing it outright.
figures.provenance: Ali S. Asani, Ecstasy and Enlightenment: The Ismaili Devotional Literature of South Asia (2002); Zawahir Moir and Christopher Shackle, Ismaili Hymns from South Asia: An Introduction to the Ginans (1992); Farhad Daftary, The Isma'ilis.

figures.gloss: 14th-century Nizari pir credited by Satpanth tradition with converting Lohana and other Hindu castes of Sindh and Punjab to Ismailism, coining the term 'Khoja' (from the Persian honorific khwaja) for the new community, founding its first jamatkhanas, and composing the largest attributed share of the Ginan corpus.
figures.facet: A community's origin story concentrated in one biography: nearly every institutional marker of the Nizari Khoja tradition — its name, its houses of worship, its devotional literature — traces to a single credited founder, though (as with many pir attributions generally) the historical Sadr al-Din is difficult to isolate cleanly from the corpus later tradition gathered under his name.
figures.provenance: Ali S. Asani, Ecstasy and Enlightenment (2002); Dominique-Sila Khan, Conversions and Shifting Identities: Ramdev Pir and the Ismailis in Rajasthan (1997); Farhad Daftary, A Short History of the Ismailis (1998).

edges.note: GOLD SEAM: The Sevener imamate descends directly from the Shi'i doctrine of divinely designated leadership, branching at Ja'far al-Sadiq's succession in 765 — proven doctrinal descent within Islam.
GOLD SEAM: The Druze faith crystallized in 1017 inside the Fatimid Ismaili da'wa around the caliph-imam al-Hakim — documented parent-child descent; the child then sealed itself off from the parent.
GOLD SEAM: Hamza ibn 'Ali built Druze doctrine with the tools of Fatimid ta'wil — the batin/zahir hermeneutic carried out of Ismailism into the new faith, then radicalized.
GOLD SEAM: The Epistles pour Plotinian emanation into Islamic form via the Arabic Plotinus corpus (the so-called Theology of Aristotle) — documented textual dependence, not mere resemblance.
GOLD SEAM: The Druze division into initiated 'uqqal and uninitiated juhhal carries forward the Ismaili grading of access to the batin — the esoteric-tier structure inherited from the parent da'wa.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: both traditions structure religious life around a protected inner truth (batin) that outsiders and even lower-ranked insiders are not permitted direct access to.

Cross-tradition seam: both are large bodies of esoteric epistles produced within the wider Fatimid-era Ismaili intellectual milieu — a shared genre and moment, not a demonstrated direct dependency of one text on the other.
Cross-tradition seam: al-Darazi operated as a da'i within the Fatimid Ismaili da'wa apparatus before his breakaway proclamation split off the movement that became the Druze.

Both are graded clerical/missionary hierarchies mediating access to a deeper truth through ascending office and successive initiation — no descent claimed, a structural analogy between an Isiac and an Islamic esoteric institution.
Founder and fortress — Hasan-i Sabbah's 1090 seizure of Alamut establishes the seat the whole Nizari state and its later doctrinal history unfold from.

Ta'lim's stress on the living Imam's authoritative teaching, framed by Hasan-i Sabbah, is the doctrinal ground the later Alamut lord Hasan 'ala dhikrihi al-salam (a different, later Hasan) stood on to proclaim the Qiyama seventy years afterward.
His Asas al-Ta'wil builds the ta'wil doctrine the batin/zahir shard describes as a matter of scriptural principle.
As chief qadi he administered the Fatimid state the da'wa's initiatory ladder had scaled into.
His legal code was codified explicitly under the reigning imams' own review, tying law directly to the living imamate.
He held the office of hujja, the da'wa's regional rank, over Khurasan.

Both build Neoplatonic emanationist cosmologies within the broader Ismaili intellectual world, though the Rasa'il's precise Ismaili affiliation is itself a live scholarly debate the parent shard already flags.

His tanzih doctrine (God beyond being and non-being) directly adapts the Plotinian One via the Arabic Plotinus corpus (the Theology of Aristotle) — the same textual channel already documented for the Rasa'il Ikhwan al-Safa.
Al-Kirmani reworks al-Sijistani's Intellect/Soul emanation scheme into a ten-intellect system influenced by al-Farabi — direct successor engagement within the same Fatimid philosophical lineage.
Al-Kirmani's epistles explicitly rebut the deification of al-Hakim the fledgling Druze movement was proclaiming — documented polemical engagement, not mere resemblance.
Hamza ibn 'Ali's proclamation of al-Hakim's divinity is the specific claim al-Kirmani's rebuttal epistles were written against — direct historical opposition between two named contemporaries.
The Resurrection was proclaimed at Alamut itself, in 1164, before the assembled Nizari community.
The Qiyama is the most literal possible application of batin declared to fully supersede zahir — doctrine taken to its outer edge.
Sadr al-Din is credited with composing the largest attributed share of the corpus and founding the jamatkhanas where it is sung.
The hymns are devotional praise and instruction addressed to the living Imam, the office the wider tradition centers on.

the Ginans' 'Das Avatar' tradition explicitly casts 'Ali and the line of Imams as the tenth and final avatar of Vishnu awaited to come — a documented, deliberate scriptural syncretism, not mere structural resemblance, that let Hindu converts recognize Ismaili doctrine inside their own cosmology.

dossier.essence: The second-largest branch of Shi'i Islam, tracing the imamate through Isma'il, son of Ja'far al-Sadiq (d. 765), and once sovereign as the Fatimid caliphate (909-1171) that founded Cairo and al-Azhar. Nizari Ismailis worldwide follow a living, present Imam — the Aga Khan — while the Tayyibi branch (the Bohra communities of South Asia and Yemen) holds its Imam in concealment, represented by the Da'i al-Mutlaq.
dossier.cosmos: Ismaili thought weds Qur'anic revelation to Neoplatonic emanation: creation proceeds from God through Intellect and Soul, and sacred history unfolds in prophetic cycles, each law-giving revelation bearing an outward letter (zahir) and an inner truth (batin). Salvation is the soul's ascent through knowledge of that inner meaning, whose authorized key is the divinely designated Imam of the age; the resurrection is read spiritually — most radically in the Nizari proclamation of the Qiyama at Alamut in 1164.
dossier.practice: Devotional life is inflected throughout by the imamate: allegiance and tithes to the Imam or his representative, graded religious instruction descending from the old missionary hierarchy (the da'wa) that once carried the faith from clandestine cells to an empire, and esoteric interpretation (ta'wil) as a discipline of study. Nizari communities gather in jamatkhanas under the Aga Khan's guidance, paired today with a large development network; Tayyibi Bohras maintain Fatimid-derived law and liturgy under the Da'i al-Mutlaq.
dossier.canon: The Qur'an, read through a distinctive Fatimid corpus — al-Qadi al-Nu'man's Da'a'im al-Islam and the ta'wil literature — together with the Rasa'il Ikhwan al-Safa adopted by the Tayyibi da'wa and, for Nizaris, the Alamut-era and later Persian tradition.
dossier.reflects: Esotericism made constitutional — the conviction that revelation carries a hidden meaning whose key is not free for the finding but vested in a living, divinely designated interpreter.
sources: Farhad Daftary, 'The Isma'ilis: Their History and Doctrines' (1990)
Heinz Halm, 'The Empire of the Mahdi: The Rise of the Fatimids' (1996)
Farhad Daftary, 'The Assassin Legends: Myths of the Isma'ilis' (1994)

links.cite: Farhad Daftary, 'The Isma'ilis: Their History and Doctrines' (1990)
Farhad Daftary, 'The Assassin Legends: Myths of the Isma'ilis' (1994)
